aliases: Tandoor Chicken Kabab with Cheese and Cashew

english: Tandoor Chicken Kabab with Cheese and Cashew

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for Murgh Chandi Kabab; closest match is chicken kebab food product (FOODON_03302051).
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Murgh Chandi Kabab; A0FJP (Chicken meat, grilled) is the closest defensible anchor. The dish includes cheese and cashew paste but the primary protein component is grilled chicken.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for Murgh Chandi Kabab or a sufficiently close tandoor chicken kebab variant.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Murgh Chandi Kabab is approximately 200 g, comprising 3–4 pieces of tandoor-grilled boneless chicken, served on a bed of spiced bell pepper.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken tikka / tandoori chicken (boneless, grilled) — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods; supplemented with USDA FoodData Central entries for cashew paste and processed cheese components
nutrient_source.organization: ICMR-NIN (Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition); USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Murgh Chandi Kabab exists in ICMR-NIN or USDA. Values are estimated from ICMR-NIN tandoori chicken (boneless, grilled) as the primary base, with upward adjustment to fat and energy to account for the cheese and cashew paste marinade components, consistent with published recipe proportions. Protein is slightly reduced relative to plain tandoori chicken due to dilution by the cheese-cashew marinade. This is a medium-confidence estimate.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed protein-fat dishes with low carbohydrate content — analogous estimate based on tandoori chicken and grilled chicken entries; University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Murgh Chandi Kabab. GI is estimated at approximately 35 (low) based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for tandoori chicken and similar grilled chicken dishes with minimal carbohydrate content. The cashew paste and cheese marinade contribute small amounts of carbohydrate and fat, which further attenuate glycemic response. GL per serving = (35 × 4.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 200 g serving) / 100 = 3.15, rounded to 3.2.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~420 mg per 100 g; ~840 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to one serving and avoid additional high-sodium accompaniments such as chutneys or pickles, as a single 200 g serving contributes approximately 840 mg sodium, representing about 37% of the recommended daily limit of 2300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content (~4.5 g per 100 g) and low estimated glycemic index (~35)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Murgh Chandi Kabab has a low estimated glycemic index and low glycemic load per serving (~3.2), making it a suitable protein-rich option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-GI accompaniments such as naan or white rice.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from cheese and cream marinade (~3.8 g per 100 g; ~7.6 g per serving) and moderate sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should be mindful of the saturated fat contributed by the cheese and cream marinade; a 200 g serving provides approximately 7.6 g saturated fat, which represents about 38% of the recommended daily limit of 20 g, and the moderate sodium content warrants attention in the context of overall daily intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Murgh Chandi Kabab (Hindi: मुर्ग चाँदी कबाब) is a refined North Indian tandoor-grilled chicken dish in which boneless chicken pieces are marinated in a white marinade of processed cheese, cashew paste, cream, cardamom, white pepper, ginger-garlic paste, and lemon juice before being cooked in a clay tandoor oven at high heat. The name 'Chandi' (meaning silver in Hindi and Urdu) refers to the pale, ivory-white colour of the finished dish, which distinguishes it from the more familiar red-hued tandoori chicken. The texture is tender and juicy with a subtle smoky char from the tandoor, and the flavour profile is mild, creamy, and aromatic with prominent cardamom and cashew notes. It is typically served on a bed of spiced bell pepper with mint chutney and lemon wedges. Nutritionally, Murgh Chandi Kabab is a high-protein, moderate-fat dish with low carbohydrate content, making it suitable for low-carbohydrate dietary patterns. The cheese and cashew paste contribute saturated fat and calories beyond plain grilled chicken. Sodium content is moderate to high due to the marinade and seasoning. The estimated glycemic index is low (~35), and the glycemic load per standard serving is low (~3.2), making it appropriate for individuals managing blood glucose. It is a popular starter at North Indian restaurants in Singapore and across the Indian subcontinent.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Murgh Chandi Kabab exists in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN tandoori chicken (boneless, grilled) with adjustments for the cheese and cashew paste marinade. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database based on similar grilled chicken dishes. Confidence is medium rather than low because the primary protein base (tandoor-grilled boneless chicken) is well-characterised in ICMR-NIN, and the marinade components are well-characterised in USDA.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from ICMR-NIN tandoori chicken (boneless, grilled) as the primary base, with upward adjustment to fat and energy to account for the cheese and cashew paste marinade. This is a medium-confidence estimate; exact values will vary by restaurant recipe. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this food category and is not reliably estimated from the marinade components. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA FoodData Central entries for grilled chicken and cashew/cheese components and should be treated as indicative. 4. GI is an analogous estimate (~35, low) based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for tandoori and grilled chicken dishes; no direct GI measurement for Murgh Chandi Kabab was found. 5. Halal status is assumed based on the nature of the dish and the restaurant context (North Indian cuisine); individual restaurant certification should be verified. 6. The dish is served at multiple North Indian restaurants in Singapore including Bawarchi, Bombay Grill, Bombay Curry And Kebabs, and Aryana.
